title: practical english usage - Part 367
content:
Who won - Smith or Fitzgibbon? (     Which wen . ..?) who, not which, for people.

When  these words arc not followed by nouns or pronouns, we  generally use forpeople 4 without      nouns:    who not Which of these coats is yours? (     What of these...?) -. ..?) ) Who and what are not normally used with o/like this in modern English.

Before another determiner (e.g. the, my, these) or a pronoun, we  use which of.

What writers do you like? What colour are your baby's eyes?

Which colour do you want - green, red, yellow or brown?

Which teacher do you like best?

and people.

Before nouns, which and what can be used to ask questions about both things and 2 determiners:        which what not What's your phone number? (     Which is your phone number?) Which language...) What language do they speak in Greenland? (More natural than When  we  are not thinking of a limited number of choices, what is preferred.

Which size do you want - small, medium or large?

What will you have?) We've got white or brown bread. Which will you have? (More natural than ...

We   prefer which when we  have a limited number of choices in mind.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section     31 vocabulary •  627  who ever, what ever, etc Whose is that car outside? ‘ Whose is this? ‘ Mine.' Whose can also be used alone, like mine, yours, etc.

Whose garden do you think looks the nicest?

Whose car is that outside?

my, your, etc.

The  question word whose can be used with a noun as a determiner, like 1 with    a  noun    or  alone 628 whose:      question        word 2 32.

For the conjunctions whoever, whatever, etc, ► Note that whose and which are not used with ever in this way.

The  expressions can also be written as single words: whoever, whatever, etc.

Why ever did I marry you?

When ever will I have time to do some shopping?

How ever did you manage to start the car? I couldn't.

What ever are you doing?

Who ever is that strange girl with George?

These expressions show surprise or difficulty in understanding something.

627 who     ever,     what      ever,     etc There is a child in the class who(m) I believe to be a musical genius.

more correct.

In cases with a following infinitive, usage is mixed, but whom is considered There is a child in this class who(m) I believe is a musical genius.

more correct, but whom is sometimes used. Another example: should use who (because it is the subject of the second verb). Who is considered (because it seems to be the object of the first following verb) or whether they was living in New Zealand, people are often unsure whether whom is possible In a sentence like He was trying to find an old school friend, who(m) he thought 3  who(m)      he   thought,         etc I have a number of American relatives, most of whom live in Texas.

This is John Perkins, whom you met at the sales conference.

when necessary (but these clauses are uncommon in informal English).

In non-identifying relative clauses (►  234), we  usually use whom as an object She married a man whom she met at a conference.

In a formal style whom is more common.

There's the man (that)/(who) we met in the pub last night.

(►   233-234   for details).

Either we  leave out the object pronoun, or we  use that or who In identifying relative clauses, (►  234), whom is unusual in an informal style.

2  relative      clauses:     the    man     (who)    we    met vocabulary •  630   why and why not Why pay more at other shops? We have the best value.

Why argue with him?  He'll never change his mind, (     Why arguing.. .?

an action is unnecessary or pointless.

Why can be followed by an infinitive without to. This structure can suggest that 3  infinitive       structures ►     303 2.

For a similar structure with how, ‘ Give me a cigarette.' ‘ Why should I?

I don't see why we should have to pay for your mistake.

The  structure can also suggest anger or refusal to do something.

I wonder why she should want to go out with me.

A  structure with why followed by should can suggest surprise.

2  Why     should       . . . ?

‘ Let's eat out this evening.' ‘ Yes, why not?
